content:
# 1 Corinthians Chapter 9 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in 1 Corinthians Chapter 9 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 9:1 — "Have I not seen Jesus our Lord?" vs. "Have I not seen Yeshua the Lord of us?"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Have I not seen Yeshua the Lord of us?" | Preserves Hebrew name form and literal possessive |
| LOGOS | "Have I not seen Jesus our Lord?" | Standard English rendering with natural possessive |
| PNEUMA | "Haven't I seen Jesus our Lord with my own eyes?" | Adds "with my own eyes" for emphatic clarity |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's "Yeshua" and "the Lord of us" are consistent with EMET's literal methodology but overly foreignizing for the consensus text. PNEUMA's "with my own eyes" is interpretively accurate (the verb implies personal sight) but adds words not present in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's expansion is interpretively defensible but exceeds the text's explicit wording.

---

## 9:2 — "the seal of my apostleship" vs. "proof that I am the Lord's apostle"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the seal of the apostleship of me you are in [the] Lord" | Hyper-literal with bracketed articles |
| LOGOS | "you are the seal of my apostleship in the Lord" | Formal equivalent with natural English |
| PNEUMA | "You yourselves are proof that I am the Lord's apostle" | Dynamic rendering; "seal" becomes "proof" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering, retaining "seal" (sphragis). PNEUMA's "proof" captures the function but loses the legal/authenticating metaphor of sealing. The seal image is important — it carries connotations of authentication and ownership that "proof" does not convey.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The metaphor of "seal" is retained for its theological richness.

---

## 9:3 — "my defense to those who would examine me" vs. "my answer to those who question my authority"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The defense of me to the [ones] examining me is this" | Literal word order with bracketed articles |
| LOGOS | "This is my defense to those who would examine me" | Formal equivalent with natural sentence structure |
| PNEUMA | "This is my answer to those who question my authority" | Interprets "examine" as "question my authority" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's "question my authority" is contextually plausible but narrows the Greek anakrinousin, which means "examine/investigate" in a broad forensic sense — not exclusively about authority.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The broader forensic sense of "examine" is preserved.

---

## 9:5 — "a believing wife" / "a sister [as] wife" / "a believing wife"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a sister [as] wife" | Literal rendering of adelphen gynaika |
| LOGOS | "a believing wife" | Interprets "sister" as "believing" (fellow-Christian) |
| PNEUMA | "a believing wife" | Same dynamic interpretation as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "a believing wife" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). "Sister" in Pauline usage is a term for a fellow believer; "sister-wife" would be opaque or misleading in English. The meaning is clearly a Christian spouse.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA. EMET's literal rendering would confuse modern readers.

---

## 9:5 — "Cephas" vs. "Peter"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Kephas" | Transliterates the Aramaic form from the Greek |
| LOGOS | "Cephas" | Standard English transliteration of the Greek Kephas |
| PNEUMA | "Peter" | Uses the more familiar name for accessibility |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Cephas" (LOGOS). Paul consistently uses Kephas in his letters, and the consensus text preserves Paul's own usage. PNEUMA's "Peter" aids recognition but erases Paul's distinctive practice.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Authorial usage is preserved.

---

## 9:6 — "right to refrain from working" vs. "have to work to support ourselves"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "do we not have a right not to work?" | Literal double negative construction |
| LOGOS | "who have no right to refrain from working for a living?" | Formal equivalent with clarifying expansion |
| PNEUMA | "who have to work to support ourselves?" | Dynamic simplification |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's simplification loses the "right" language (exousia) that is the controlling concept of the chapter. The verse is about rights, not obligations.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The "right" vocabulary is essential to the chapter's argument.

---

## 9:7 — "tends a flock" vs. "cares for a flock of sheep"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "tends a flock and does not drink of the milk of the flock" | Literal with repeated "flock" |
| LOGOS | "tends a flock without getting some of the milk" | Concise formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "What shepherd cares for a flock of sheep and isn't allowed to drink some of the milk?" | Adds "shepherd" and "sheep" explicitly; adds "isn't allowed" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's additions ("shepherd," "sheep," "isn't allowed") clarify but exceed the Greek, which uses only poimainei ("tends/shepherds") without specifying "sheep."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Concision without loss of meaning.

---

## 9:8 — "on merely human authority" vs. "according to man" vs. "my own opinion"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "according to man" | Literal kata anthropon |
| LOGOS | "on merely human authority" | Interpretive expansion of the idiom |
| PNEUMA | "this isn't merely my own opinion" | Dynamic paraphrase making Paul the subject |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's "according to man" is opaque. PNEUMA personalizes the statement ("my own opinion"), but Paul is making a broader distinction between human reasoning and divine law.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The idiomatic expansion clarifies without distortion.

---

## 9:11 — "material things" vs. "fleshly [things]" vs. "physical food and drink"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the fleshly [things]" | Literal sarkika — "fleshly/carnal" |
| LOGOS | "material things" | Renders sarkika as "material" |
| PNEUMA | "a harvest of physical food and drink" | Expands into concrete terms with harvest metaphor |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "material things" (LOGOS). EMET's "fleshly" carries misleading connotations in English (sinful/carnal). PNEUMA's "physical food and drink" over-specifies — sarkika includes financial support, not just food.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. "Material" captures the intended contrast with "spiritual" without negative overtones.

---

## 9:12 — "rightful claim" / "right" / "right to be supported"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "this right over you" | Literal exousia with "over" |
| LOGOS | "this rightful claim on you" | "Rightful claim" interprets exousia |
| PNEUMA | "If you support others who preach to you, shouldn't we have an even greater right to be supported?" | Full dynamic expansion |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "rightful claim on you." PNEUMA's full paraphrase, while clear, adds "who preach to you" and "to be supported," which are interpretive additions.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Balances clarity and fidelity.

---

## 9:14 — "commanded" vs. "directed" vs. "ordered"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "directed" | Renders dietaxen as "directed" |
| LOGOS | "commanded" | Stronger rendering of the same verb |
| PNEUMA | "ordered" | Even stronger authoritative language |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "commanded" (LOGOS). The verb dietaxen carries the force of an authoritative directive. EMET's "directed" is slightly weak; PNEUMA's "ordered" is acceptable but "commanded" better conveys the Lord's authority without sounding bureaucratic.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. "Commanded" captures the verb's authoritative force.

---

## 9:15 — "my ground for boasting" vs. "my right to boast"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "my boast no one will empty" | Hyper-literal with unusual English construction |
| LOGOS | "have anyone deprive me of my ground for boasting" | Formal equivalent preserving "ground for boasting" |
| PNEUMA | "lose my right to boast about preaching without charge" | Dynamic expansion clarifying the content of the boast |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's specification of what Paul boasts about ("preaching without charge") is accurate but adds interpretive content. EMET's construction is unnatural in English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:17 — "stewardship" vs. "sacred trust"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a stewardship has been entrusted [to me]" | Literal oikonomia |
| LOGOS | "I am still entrusted with a stewardship" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "God has given me this sacred trust" | Dynamic rendering adding "God" and "sacred" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's "sacred trust" captures the weight but adds "sacred" and names "God" as subject, neither of which is explicit in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The term "stewardship" is retained for its Pauline theological resonance.

---

## 9:19 — "servant to all" vs. "enslaved myself to all" vs. "slave to all people"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I enslaved myself to all" | Literal edoulosa — "enslaved" |
| LOGOS | "I have made myself a servant to all" | Softens "enslaved" to "servant" |
| PNEUMA | "I have become a slave to all people to bring many to Christ" | Retains "slave" but adds purpose clause |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "servant to all." While EMET's "enslaved" is literally accurate (edoulosa is from doulos, "slave"), "servant" is the standard English rendering in this context and avoids the loaded connotations of "enslaved" in English. PNEUMA's addition of "to bring many to Christ" is interpretive expansion.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The rendering balances accuracy and readability.

---

## 9:20 — "Jews" / "Ioudaioi" / "Jews"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Ioudaioi" / "Ioudaios" | Transliterates the Greek without translating |
| LOGOS | "Jews" / "a Jew" | Standard English translation |
| PNEUMA | "the Jews" / "a Jew" with full expansion | Standard translation with explanatory expansion |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Jews" (LOGOS). EMET's transliteration preserves the original term but creates unnecessary opacity in the consensus text. The term Ioudaioi in Paul straightforwardly refers to the Jewish people.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:20-21 — "win" vs. "gain" vs. "bring to Christ"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "gain" | Literal kerdeso — "gain/profit" |
| LOGOS | "win" | Standard dynamic equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "bring...to Christ" | Interprets "gain" as conversion language |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "win" (LOGOS). EMET's "gain" is literally precise but sounds transactional. PNEUMA's "bring to Christ" is theologically accurate but adds a christological specification not in the Greek. "Win" captures the sense naturally.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:22 — "win the weak" vs. "bring the weak to Christ"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "gain the weak" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "win the weak" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "bring the weak to Christ" | Dynamic expansion |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "win the weak" (LOGOS), consistent with the resolution for vv.20-21.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:23 — "share with them in its blessings" vs. "become a fellow sharer of it" vs. "share in its blessings"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "that I may become a fellow sharer of it" | Literal synkoinonos |
| LOGOS | "that I may share with them in its blessings" | Adds "blessings" for clarity |
| PNEUMA | "I do everything to spread the Good News and share in its blessings" | Adds "spread the Good News" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Blessings" is a reasonable clarification of what is shared. PNEUMA adds "spread" language not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:24 — "in a race" / "in a stadium" / "in a race"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [ones] running in a stadium" | Preserves "stadium" from the Greek |
| LOGOS | "in a race all the runners run" | Renders stadion as "race" |
| PNEUMA | "in a race everyone runs" | Dynamic simplification |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "in a race." The English "race" captures the meaning; "stadium" in English connotes a structure rather than an event.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:25 — "wreath" / "crown" / "prize"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a perishable crown" | Renders stephanos as "crown" |
| LOGOS | "a perishable wreath" | Specifies the athletic garland |
| PNEUMA | "a prize that will fade away" | Generalizes to "prize" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "wreath" (LOGOS). The stephanos at the Isthmian Games was a pine wreath, not a royal crown. "Wreath" is more historically precise. PNEUMA's "prize" loses the concrete image.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Historical precision serves the athletic metaphor.

---

## 9:26 — "aimlessly" / "uncertainly" / "with purpose in every step"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "not as uncertainly" | Literal adelos |
| LOGOS | "I do not run aimlessly" | Renders positively for clarity |
| PNEUMA | "I run with purpose in every step. I am not just shadowboxing." | Full dynamic expansion with "shadowboxing" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's "shadowboxing" is vivid and contextually equivalent to "beating the air" but introduces a modern term. EMET's "uncertainly" is accurate but less idiomatic.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:27 — "discipline my body and keep it under control" vs. "discipline the body of me and make [it] a slave" vs. "discipline my body like an athlete"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I discipline the body of me and make [it] a slave" | Literal hypopiazo and doulagogo |
| LOGOS | "I discipline my body and keep it under control" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "I discipline my body like an athlete, training it to do what it should" | Dynamic expansion with simile |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's "make it a slave" is literally accurate for doulagogo but stark. PNEUMA's "like an athlete" adds a simile not in the text. LOGOS balances accuracy and readability.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| EMET literalism vs. LOGOS/PNEUMA clarity | 14 | EMET's hyper-literal forms (possessives, bracketed articles, transliterated names) consistently diverged from LOGOS and PNEUMA |
| PNEUMA expansions | 12 | PNEUMA regularly added interpretive words, phrases, or modern equivalents not in the Greek |
| Word choice (theological terms) | 6 | Key terms like "seal," "stewardship," "wreath," "gain/win" produced three-way divergence |
| Name rendering | 2 | Yeshua/Jesus, Kephas/Cephas/Peter divergences |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 9 shows the typical three-agent dynamic. EMET stays close to the Greek morphology and syntax, producing English that is transparent to the source but often awkward. LOGOS occupies the middle ground with polished formal equivalence. PNEUMA prioritizes reader comprehension, frequently adding explanatory phrases ("with my own eyes," "like an athlete," "to bring many to Christ"). SOPHIA's consensus consistently favored LOGOS for this chapter, as its balance of accuracy and clarity best served the argument-heavy rhetoric of Paul's apostolic defense. PNEUMA's additions, while often theologically accurate, exceeded the text's explicit content.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
